era_context: Two years before the Persian invasion of Greece. The Achaemenid Empire is at its height under Xerxes I, who has recently consolidated power after his father Darius I's death. Greek city-states are becoming aware of the Persian threat but have not yet formed a unified alliance. In China, Confucius is in his late 60s and continues his teaching. India sees the consolidation of early kingdoms.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Most of Asia beyond China and India
Specific events in Greece for this year are poorly documented

events.title: Xerxes I consolidates power in Persia
events.description: Xerxes I, who became king in 486 BCE after his father Darius I's death, was consolidating his power and authority in 482 BCE. Early in his reign, Xerxes faced a rebellion in Egypt (c. 486 BCE) and suppressed it. By 482 BCE, he had established firm control over the empire and was planning the major military campaign against Greece. His reign was characterized by the maintenance of Persian imperial power and the pursuit of expansionist policies.
events.certainty_note: Xerxes' reign dates and general activities are well-attested in Persian and Greek sources. The specific events of 482 BCE are less detailed.

events.title: Confucius at age 69
events.description: Confucius, born in 551 BCE, was approximately 69 years old in 482 BCE. He continued his teaching and philosophical work in the state of Lu. According to traditional accounts, he was in his final years of active teaching before his death in 479 BCE. His disciples were actively recording his teachings and engaging in philosophical discussions with him. This period represents the mature phase of his philosophical development.
events.certainty_note: Confucius' life dates are traditional; no contemporary records document his specific activities in 482 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the sparse documentation for 482 BCE means that many events of potential significance may have occurred but are not recorded in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: 482 BCE is poorly documented compared to 480 BCE. No major events are recorded for this year in Greek, Persian, Indian, or Chinese sources. The year is primarily significant as part of the lead-up to the Persian invasion of Greece. The lack of specific events reflects the limitations of ancient historical documentation—many years, especially in the pre-Classical period, leave little trace in surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Xerxes' consolidation of power in 482 BCE enabled the planning and execution of the Greek invasion in 480 BCE.
